nativeRange.habitat: Creeks and tributaries away from the main river channels, inhabiting both white and black water environments; soft, often acidic water with sandy substrate, minimal aquatic vegetation, and fallen leaf litter

decorPreferences: dried oak or beech leaf litter
dense planting (Java fern, mosses, Cryptocoryne) to provide cover
dimmed or subdued lighting to enhance coloration

diet.notes: Readily accepts dried flakes and micro-granules. For optimal coloration, supplement regularly with small live and frozen foods: bloodworm, daphnia, and brine shrimp nauplii. In the wild, feeds on small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, and plant matter.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra, rummy nose tetra)
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (fin-nipping risk, especially in small groups)
very small nano fish that may be harassed

summary: A lively, hardy, and colourful small tetra from Brazil's São Francisco basin. Males display copper-gold body colouration with distinctive silver-tipped fins. One of the few characids lacking an adipose fin. Excellent choice for community tanks when kept in groups of 6 or more.
careNotes: The Silver Tip Tetra is a robust and adaptable species from the Rio São Francisco drainage in Minas Gerais, Brazil, where it inhabits shaded creeks and tributaries with both black and white water conditions. Commercially available specimens are almost entirely captive-bred. A minimum group of 6 is strongly recommended — individual fish or small groups tend to show fin-nipping behaviour toward tankmates, but this diminishes significantly in larger groups of 10 or more as the fish focus intraspecific activity on one another. Provide a tank of at least 70 litres with a 60 cm minimum footprint. Best colours are displayed under slightly dimmed lighting against a dark substrate or heavy planting. While adaptable to a wide pH range (6.0–8.0), soft to moderately hard water (1–10 dGH) is preferred and reflects natural conditions in the São Francisco basin. The genus Hasemania is distinctive within Characidae for lacking an adipose fin — a small fleshy lobe between the dorsal and caudal fins present in most of the family; its absence can be used to confirm identification. Note on taxonomy: SeriouslyFish places Hasemania nana in family Characidae; FishBase (as of 2026) lists it under Acestrorhamphidae (subfamily Stichonodontinae), reflecting ongoing characiform reclassification following molecular phylogenetic work. Characidae is used here as the more widely recognised trade classification.
breedingNotes: Readily bred in captivity. Condition breeders with live and frozen foods prior to spawning. Spawning can be achieved in a small dedicated tank (approx. 45 cm) furnished with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Pair spawning: raise temperature slightly to 28–30°C with near-neutral, soft water. Group spawning is also effective. Eggs are scattered and hatch within 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming 3–4 days post-hatch. Remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation. Feed fry infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, progressing to microworm and brine shrimp nauplii as they grow.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hasemania nana
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided: adult size (5 cm max SL), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–20 dH), tank minimum (70 L / 60 cm), diet (flakes + live/frozen), minimum group size (6, preferably 10+), breeding method (pair and group spawning, 24–36 h hatch, fry free-swim 3–4 days), native range (Rio São Francisco, Brazil), habitat (creeks and tributaries, black and white water), biotope decor notes (sandy substrate, driftwood, leaf litter, dimmed lighting), conspecific fin-nipping in small groups, taxonomy (Characidae), and the notable adipose-fin absence as a genus-level diagnostic.

additional.name: FishBase — Hasemania nana
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max size (3.8 cm SL male/unsexed — diverges from SeriouslyFish 5 cm; used 5 cm per SF as the aquarium-accepted morphological max, with 3 cm as practical minimum adult size seen in trade), temperature (22–28°C — used over SF 23–28°C as the wider reported range), pH (6.0–8.0, matches), hardness (5–19 dH, matches SF 5–20 dH), native range (São Francisco basin, Minas Gerais, Brazil — confirms). Taxonomy divergence: FishBase lists family as Acestrorhamphidae (subfamily Stichonodontinae), whereas SeriouslyFish uses Characidae. This reflects post-Lima & Gerhard (2001) molecular reclassification of some characiforms. Characidae retained here as the conventional aquarium-trade classification; divergence documented in careNotes.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Hasemania nana (breeding and behaviour detail)
additional.notes: Second pass for breeding and behaviour detail. Confirmed: spawning temperature 28–30°C for pair spawning, infusoria required as first fry food, lifespan not stated on page (3–5 year estimate derived from general small-characin trade knowledge). Conspecific aggression note confirmed — alpha individuals can target other tetras in undersized groups.

fish.breedingNotes: Readily spawned in a dedicated 45 cm breeding tank with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, slightly raised temperature (28–30°C), soft slightly acidic water. Eggs hatch 24–36 h; fry free-swim in 3–4 days. Remove adults post-spawn. Feed fry infusoria then microworm/brine shrimp nauplii.
